pick.rationale: Michigan State enters as a legitimate top-10 team with an SRS ranking of 10th nationally and the 8th-toughest schedule in college basketball, while North Dakota State sits 131st in SRS with a schedule strength ranked 280th—the Bison haven't faced a single top-115 KenPom team all season. The Spartans' 25-7 record against Big Ten competition dwarfs NDSU's 27-7 mark against the Summit League, and Tom Izzo's unmatched 28 consecutive NCAA tournament appearances provides a significant coaching edge. The 16.5-point spread reflects the massive gap between these programs, and while NDSU set a school record for wins, their offensive numbers are inflated by weak competition and they lack the defensive prowess to slow down a Michigan State team that ranks 49th in offensive efficiency against elite competition.

Roster attrition is a factor during the postseason in any sport. Optimistically, college basketball’s shorter regular season should mean the title picture is less altered by injuries than, say, the NBA. That is not necessarily the case this season, though. The injury bug has recently bitten two No. 1 seeds (Duke and Michigan), and a third (Arizona) played through a flurry of nagging injuries late in the season.
